channel.title: Stories by Matt Everard on Medium
channel.description: Stories by Matt Everard on Medium

image.title: Stories by Matt Everard on Medium

item.title: The clock, the bell, the whiteboard and the chicken.
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
The clock, the bell, the whiteboard and the chicken. How engagement devices help create teams on a liveaboard scuba boat

16 strangers. 14 crew. 12 nationalities. 19 dives. 7 days

Two years ago, I didn’t know such things existed. We were in India at the time, and I was planning a fun spring break trip for my high school aged sons and landed on scuba. We went to the Andamans, the islands 1400 km off the coast of the subcontinent, and did 10 dives in 5 days from land. But from that point, I knew I would have to try a liveabord. This year, I hit my greed limit (i.e. the upper bound of the range you need to live for the year) and decided to stack up a few adventures in SE Asia instead of decorating trees and wrapping gifts

“Scuba is more a meditation than a sport” Jonathan, Captain of The Junk

And a wee bit of a dangerous one — not crazy downhill mountain bike dangerous — but if you fuck up at 30m your spouse will be reading the fine print in your life insurance policy about extreme sports

I’ve never done a cruise. I read A Surprisingly Fun Thing I Will Never Do Again ,and the matter was resolved for me. A liveaboard is the anti-cruise. It’s a working boat with duties for all. The boat isn’t that big, either. We’re talking 31m long for 30 people. And yet it felt adequate, spacious even, due to the high degree of teamwork baked into the system

The boat captain is not a traditional military type. He’s got to fold in some fun-making and hospitality into the role while making sure no one dies. How does he fast-track our teams through the Tuckman model? With a sense of humor, a clock, a bell, a whiteboard, and a chicken

The Clock

There’s only one clock on board, prominently placed in the main cabin. You could see it from every angle. We watched the clock because the clock was god. It told you when you were going to wake up, dive, eat, have a beer. There’s only one way to squeeze four dives in a day: stay on schedule

The Bell

When rung, everyone comes to the cabin. Period. Meals. Dive briefings. Wake up. Person-over-board. Anything important got the bell. Anyone can ring it, but only Bee rings it with style

The Whiteboard

Each day was decomposed here. This was your guide for the next 24 hours. No one need ask “When are we diving next?” If they did, the captain would utter no words and just point to the board

The Chicken

People make mistakes. You forget your mask, and the dive dinghy has to return to the main ship to get it for you, delaying your team. Most of these mistakes are self-inflicted. A lack of attention paid to your process for getting ready. Too much chit-chat with your buddy. Whatever. If this was you, you got the chicken attached to your tank for the next dive. Anyone could assign the chicken to another diver. This kept us on our toes. No one wanted the chicken. And no one got it. It hung prominently next to the wetsuits, taunting us all. We spoke of things as “chicken-able offenses.” We stayed alert at the most critical time of the dive — the lead-up to when you will be underwater

Engagement Devices Form Scaffolding for New Teams

Each of these are:

  • low tech and low cost — easily replaceable and require no training
  • simple — did not require a manual to use or understand
  • highly “visible” — ask any of the divers years from now where the bell was located and they will be able to tell you
  • transcend potential language barriers — international teams need as little reliance on one language as possible (yeah, the board uses words, but it’s hard to misunderstand “dive briefing”)
  • playful — the clock developed a persona, the bell ringing was done with a dance, the chicken is a chicken

Office Culture is Nearly Devoid of Engagement Devices

This point needs no belabouring. One clear benefit of the agile movement is a focus on humanizing the workplace. People are not resources. Meetings need to be interactive. Learn by doing. Play is not a four-letter word. When you use engagement devices in your office teams, e.g. tossing a ball to move the conversation, ringing bells for demos, funny hats, you see improvements

Do yourself a favor — go shop for an engagement device for your team this weekend and see what your team does with it

item.title: Is Existential Crisis a Sine Qua Non for Large Agile Transformation?
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
Let’s think of a large agile transformation as very dangerous, complex, experiment, comparable to a Crispr attempt to change the DNA of big dinos into small mammals. It’s not going to be easy. It may not even be possible and surely will require new ways of working, inventions and a culture of discovery coupled with a fanatical collective determination to succeed

“Ahh, you are being too dramatic Mr Everard! We have succeeded in many LeSS and SaFE transformations!” say a small army of well-paid consultants. To wit: I call bullshit. Oh, is that too harsh? Let me explain

Agility is Release Cadence

Sutherland and Schwaber, in Software in 30 Days, give us this:

Agility is a measure of one’s ability to take advantage of an opportunity. We can turn on a dime, immediately launch bold initiatives, and manage our risks. We can make our competitors weep when they wake up in the morning, and we can please our newly found customers

In the hundreds of conversations I’ve had over the “essence of agile,” the smart kids, the folks whose business sponsors buy them flowers and chocolate, insist on focusing on the “one metric that matters” — release cadence. Wait haters — there are other metrics that matter (MTTR, quality, etc), but I’m taking those as a given — hygiene factors. If you can release multiple times a day into production, you have a rock-solid delivery pipeline that doesn’t rely on humans to inspect for quality and has automated roll-backs.

Let’s Examine the Current State of Enterprise IT

Today’s MNC IT shop is

  • Focused on stability over innovation
  • “Just don’t break anything”
  • Terrified of change — most large changes fail

So there are many, many layers of decision makers: exec sponsors, planning committees, spreadsheet jockeys, financial analysts, program managers, all of this to determine what *should be built*

Then there are the requirements, build, and test phases, followed by BAT and release weekend. This is to ensure availability and quality. We need all these phases because we are relying on 90’s tech and manual processes

It’s not uncommon to take 18 months from idea to production in big IT shops

Welcome to the transformation

Globally nearly all large finservs are going through “transformation.” Often “digital” or “agile” or “devops” is prepended. What does this mean?

Your execs know:

  • The company is a dinosaur
  • The meteor (disruption) is coming
  • Only small furry mammals will survive (read: highly experimental, passionate about customer experience, able to respond to change in days not months or years)

This organizational DNA change, dino-to-mammal, is complex in the extreme. I see 3 possible states of being for big bank IT in the next few years:

  1. Still a brachiosaurus — tried to transform but failed. Too painful, too many political problems, visionary leaders move on, middle-management revolts, new way of working creates service quality problems causing the business to revolt. This will be the majority of big banks
  2. Made it to Velociraptor — successfully implemented a scaled agile framework and achieved a 20% improvement in performance/quality from the IT organization. Costs are down considerably compared to peers. Best of class IT organization wins the CIO awards and bonuses. Still has a cost structure way out of whack with a disruptor. Change is much faster than before but idea-to-release is many months, not days
  3. Hair and tits — an “enterprise IT” that looks more like AMZN than a bank. This will require incredible fortitude and leadership as the organization itself is has an immune system built to reject the change. Imagine convincing bankers that the world they’ve known for decades, that has made them rich, is about to explode. That’s not even the hardest part. It’s dismantling the ossified fiefdoms that have evolved to support the current state

I’ve seen some attempts in-progress in the wild, usually some form of “create your own Uber” inside the bank. Build or buy a platform and cannibalize your existing business. These companies, if they can execute, will decimate their competition. It won’t be a fair fight. Fees will be lower, quality of service higher, digital platform offerings far, far superior

Transformation is possible if you partner with the business

I have seen a few examples of true transformation in big bank IT. One group in particular really stands out. 200 engineers across 3 regions who moved from a handful of releases per year to thousands. How’d they do it? They started by enlisting their global MD — a very big dog to have in your fight — which gave them the political capital to fight battles with the lovely souls in change management, risk, security. Key takeaways:

  1. Excellent technical leadership who were so persistent and fearless as to be pugnacious shit-disturbers. They were awesome and were not afraid to fight stupid
  2. Tight partnership with the business. An MD actually attended retros and played along as part of the team. At first I thought having the boss in the retro was (potentially) a bad idea, but after listening to the team, I saw that the MD understood the rules of engagement and wasn’t acting like an old school boss. This made the team feel empowered and increased the trust and transparency
  3. “Challenge the team to deliver more frequently. Everything else will follow,” was the regular refrain of technical team lead. This clean, clear message was key to the team’s cohesion and focus
  4. “You get what you measure” was the mantra of the team — so they kept their eyes on production quality, release cadence, and not a lot else. Yeah, they had other numbers but their heart was in those two

Final thoughts

There are a lot of articles on “Why agile transformations fail” — many of them focus on tactics rather than asking, “Did this company approach the change as existential necessity?” Do the CEO and Board believe the company will cease to exist in a few years if they don’t dramatically reshape in short order? In other words, do they see the meteor in the sky?

item.title: Packing Tips for Your 2-Year Overseas Assignment
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
First off, everyone is lying to you about the timeframes for delivery. Everything will take 2–3x longer. What follows is some practical advice for dealing with your precious goods

You’ll likely get four places to put everything you own: suitcases, air shipment, sea shipment, and LTS (long term storage)

Suitcases

  • You’ll get at least two @ 32kg per. That’s a lot of weight. It’s pretty hard to make a bag that heavy with soft goods. Why does the airline give such a large allowance? B/c expats put crazy shit in their Samsonites
  • Lesson: Don’t pack like you normally do with all clothes and shoes. Instead add key items from each room in your house. That’s the easiest way to describe it. So bring that french press if that’s your fancy, kitchen tools you use daily but a hotel suite will not have (manual citrus press, juicer, the Maybach of blenders), enough of your toiletries to last 3 months, office supplies, a mini-printer perhaps (India, where we were, is still very paper-based), your Sonos, airport base station, spices and special condiments that you love, sausages (you laugh, but it’s true, every seasoned expat will have have cured meats in his luggage). Walk around your house and tag items that you use everyday. Pack as many of those as you can. Plan to live out of your suitcases for 90 days. No joke
  • Use duffel bags for half of your allowance — huge carrying capacity and condense to nothing in your new flat
  • As for suitcase clothes/shoes: bring a pair of shoes from each category. Boots, sandals, trekkers, runners, dress, office, sport-specific. I forgot to pack trekkers on the way to India (I’m not going hiking in the monsoon!). Yeah, we went hiking in the monsoon. Also went to China on a man-adventure. Basically ruined a fine pair of Varvatos dress boots b/c there was “no fuckin way I’m buying a pair of trekkers when I already own some”
  • Buy two really nice kitchen knives that come in box. Your mobile self will love you. You won’t see your other kitchen goods for 3 months. Bad knives are synonymous with rental suites and nothing in the food prep world sucks like cutting with a bad blade

Air shipment

Timeline: they tell you 7 days, but it’s 30 days door to door

  • You have some extra rules here depending on country — e.g. when exiting India our shipper looked at our health supplements (e.g. charcoal pills) and asked, “Where’s the Rx?” You reply: These are over-the-counter. “The customs guys won’t know that. This needs to go into Sea container.” Customs in some countries is an entrepreneurial affair, and the inspectors are looking for a little baksheesh. You can always risk it, but it’s not advised
  • You’ll get a weight limit on the air shipment. Like all good westerners we confused more with better and tried to max out the allowance. That was stupid. On both ends of our trip we didn’t use much from the air shipment. We were in temporary housing (800sf apartments) until the sea shipment with our beds arrived, so what are we going to do with that fancy office chair? Best advice I can give is put the rest of the tagged items from your initial house walk-about. Stuff of daily use. Keep the amount small so you can absorb it into your tiny apartment for months 2 and 3. Maybe your kid’s desktop computer and xbox

Sea Shipment — e.g. what do you take and what do leave behind?

Timeline: they tell you 30 days but it’s 90+ door to door

  • We have some really nice couches — custom jobs — and bringing them was a vexing question during the pack. Will they get ruined? Will they fit in our new place (which hasn’t been chosen)? Or will they be an albatross that will limit our apartment selection (b/c they are big). And we have a room-sized Persian rug — do we bring it? Again, the albatross question. We brought both and were very happy we did. They limited our apartment selection (we could have lived closer to work and a smaller place), but it also forced us into a new-build (with no furniture). So my advice is this: pack everything you LOVE, everything that makes your life good. Don’t put it in storage where you may not see it again for a very long time

LTS — AKA furniture purgatory

  • This is for heavy, bulky, half-broken, 8 year-old shit that you should have given away. Items that will, upon opening the crate some years later, make you question your consumerist ways. But your employer will pay for it, so why force the emotional discomfort of getting rid of it. Let your garbage age in a warehouse for years. We all do it. When I ponder how much junk like this is in storage across the US and Canada it blows my mind. My advice: give away anything you can, especially tech that gets better and cheaper every month. I gave my buddy a 42" flat-screen. He put it in his office. It feels good to give things away. A win-win. The only things of value we have in LTS are an antique buffet we feared would break in transit, a KitchenAid stand-mixer and other nice 110v kitchen equipment, photo albums that we should have packed, bikes we should stored w/ friends, and my son’s weight set
  • Lesson: LTS is not setup for you to retrieve anything mid-term. It’s a vault that remains sealed until repat-time. We didn’t realize this and tried (in vain) to get our bikes out during a summer trip home. “Sir, you can maybe come at the end of the week and it will cost $150/per hour while you are in the warehouse to cover the cost of the forklift operators” — where are you located? “Out in the middle of nowhere” (read: rent a truck). We never got those bikes

A Final Storage Option

  • Leave some things with friends or in a personal storage locker in your home country — one of my biggest regrets was to put my bicycles and snowsport gear in LTS. You will come home for visits. Everyone does. And you will repatriate someday and will be in temp housing for 3 months waiting for your sea shipment. During that time you won’t have access to your LTS (they will come one day and unload the entire container at whatever address you provide) and will be living like a tourist in your home city. When you pack, prepare a future care package for your visiting self
  • Lesson: Take cold-weather clothing on your assignment (even if you are going to warm location). You will go on vacation to a cold place in the course of your two years in the tropics. Guarantee it. Do you really want to buy $100 gloves in Pune?

One of the best, completely unintended consequences of this experience was to purge our lives of the cruft of daily living. There are so many good books on this, on pairing down your life, getting rid of anything that doesn’t give you joy to touch, but it’s really, really hard to do. Unless you are forced. But I can tell you that we all loved the streamlined life of fewer possessions. My sons can move around the globe with only a backpack and small suitcase. That’s a lot of freedom

If you liked this, click the💚 below so other people will see this here on Medium.

item.title: Agile Matt’s YouTube Mondays List
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
“The internet is good in India,” I was told many times. When arriving I learned that “good” was meant in comparison to what they had a decade ago. Bit rates are good enough to stream video, but most locals buy a bandwidth-limited package. And it’s not cheap. We bought a 50GB package our first month for a $100 USD. It was used up in 3 days. Four of us, eight devices. Poof!

So take it for granted that your teammates don’t spend evenings burning bits on youtube. Do them a favor and show them some the interweb’s best on the company dime

I chose to kick-start the week with a fun learning video. Here’s a short list. I showed these just before lunch each Monday to the entire floor. Most are sub-20 minutes, fun, and mentally energizing

  • Hitler in a sprint review (4 mins)
  • Sh*t bad SMs say (3 mins)
  • Agile Product Ownership in a Nutshell — Kniberg (16 mins)
  • Spotify Engineer Culture part 1 & 2 — Kniberg (13 mins each)
  • Humans need not apply (15 mins)
  • JIRA in a nutshell (4 mins)
  • GOTO 2015 • Why Scaling Agile Doesn’t Work • Jez Humble (51 mins — just edit it to include best parts)
  • The art of doing twice as much in half the time | Jeff Sutherland | TEDxAix (18 mins)
  • Living in flow — the secret of happiness with Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi at Happiness & Its Causes 2014 (24 mins)
  • The puzzle of motivation | Dan Pink (18 mins)
  • How great leaders inspire action | Simon Sinek (18 mins)

And just for fun (point and shoot — all amazing)

  • Kurzgesagt — In a Nutshell
  • CGP Grey

Also, amazing short videos — can substitute for SM training course in a pinch

  • Scrum Training Series

item.title: Part 1 of Lessons Learned from Two Years in India as Agile & DevOps Transformation Lead for a Very…
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
Part 1 of Lessons Learned from Two Years in India as Agile & DevOps Transformation Lead for a Very Big MNC

I’ve just wrapped up two years in India as Agile & DevOps Transformation Lead for one the planet’s biggest banks. I want to share some of the fun lessons and tactics behind agile transformation at scale in my little corner of India. I’m not going into strategy or business engagement yet.

Step 1 : Let’s start with chai and BBQ Nation

  • Your first day will be very social. Count on a two hour lunch and so many chai breaks your belly will hurt. Indians are some of the warmest souls on earth. You are (they will make you feel) more special here than you have ever been. Soak in this sunshine. If you’ve had a couple of abusive years in corporate hell, consider this karmic balance. Chat up the big boss, his (and it’s almost certainly a male although that is changing) managers, technical leads, keeners — especially the keeners as they will be your foot soldiers later. Listen and learn for the first 6–8 weeks. Don’t rock any boats. There’s plenty of time for that later. Understand the pain points and how the sausage really gets made. It will shock you
  • Spend lots of time with the PM community — you don’t know it yet, but they hold the keys to your success. PMs in India, Yami G once told me, are regarded as the “land owners,” and dev teams are the farmers. It helps to have the gentry on your side. Win their trust by listening. They are more keen to go agile than you expect. Many of them want self-organizing teams and see being a SM as good for their CV
  • Big shout-outs here to Pankaj, Alok, and Sushil — my PM brethren who carried the torch for change
  • Build a library of physical books that is very visible. Loan out books freely. Get extra copies. Send the receipts to the big boss. Start a book club or discuss regularly over lunch. Publicly praise those who read. Call them thought-leaders. Generate interest in ideas. Quote the books in your town halls. Hold them up for people to see
  • Extend your network. Get lunch with any big boss. It’s pretty hierarchical here. Having relationships with powerful people pays dividends. Plus, you are exotic — one of the few expats on campus. You are more sexy than normal
  • But you will also want to make a lot of friends outside as well. They will be able to answer all sorts of questions you’ll have over the next two years. I was fortunate enough to meet some A-listers like Naresh Jain (leader of Agile India conferences and AgileFAQs), Yami G, Kamlesh Ravlani (Agile for Growth), and the team at ThoughtWorks including Manoj Ramchandani, Sriram Narayan, and Vishal Bhalerao — all indispensable sounding boards and counselors

Step 2: Plant some seeds

  • Start selling the vision in language that addresses pain points you have discovered. Create a 20 minute “Why, How, What” for agile. Sell it personally in mini-town halls. My audience had zero experience with agile so it was important to keep the message simple and consistent
  • I did 30 min sessions with 15–20 people per. After I did my song and dance, they had 8 mins Q&A and we finished with a challenge to them to come up with answers to the question: “What does agile mean to me?” I know, that sounds pretty stupid. It just happened on the spot. But it was so warmly received and created such positive energy that it stuck. I challenged each group to generate more than the last. They had great quotes like “agile is not new — it’s the way humans have be building things for millenia” and “embrace change, don’t fight it.” After 30 of these sessions, I had a lot of home-grown marketing material to plaster across the walls
  • Agile 1,2,3: I like to move fast but knew we couldn’t just flip a large department overnight. What we could do was try becoming agile in 3 steps. First, every team started doing daily standups. Then team-level agile (water-scrum-fall) followed by “true agile” (where the business owns the backlog)
  • Teams loved the standups. No one was forced to adopt them, they just enjoyed it. For the most part
  • Lesson: make sure your folks understand that they should only attend one standup per day. Case in point: We had managers wanting standups with their directs (how can that be bad?) and then directs having to go to distinct standups for each project they were on. Three standups a day! Horrible. But if you make a blanket “no more than one standup” rule the teams will self-organize and figure out a solution (e.g. Sachin will be my surrogate)
  • Lead by example: The senior management team were the first to adopt standups and they were religious about it. There were good days and bad, but everyone now wonders how they managed to survive in the old system (answer: lots of work fell on a handful of key people the big boss trusted)
  • Choose a goldilocks pilot in parallel to the selling. Something that is not too hot, nor too cold. It needs to be big enough to be a meaningful win but not too big as to be a guaranteed failure. You will be like a mother hen to this project. If it fails, pack your bags
  • Ensure the goldilocks team members are having fun and that the on-shore PO is ecstatic — willing to go on record about what she has experienced. Have team members give testimonials to the floor (i.e. all staff) about what they’ve learned. People will line up to be a part of the next tranche of projects running Scrum
  • Emphasize that agile is a more engaging, empowering, and energizing way to work. When one of my colleagues shouted, “It is more human!” I knew they were getting it

Step 3: Grow the Thing, Carefully

  • If you are like most companies, you are still project/program based and HR and the Target Operating Model haven’t caught up to your agile transformation, so pick a program with a steady funding stream and fight for a ring-fenced, co-located team. Die on this hill, my friend. If you try to scale with transient teams, working on multiple projects simultaneously, physically located across many regions, take a pass — your chance of success is near nil. Have your boss call me if you get any guff
  • Hopefully you now have a cadre of x-functional, co-located, ring-fenced teams working on a single backlog. Now go hire a coach. Get a few. You are going to be too busy to handle this alone
  • Industry standard ratios for coaches are 1 coach per 2 teams (each being a two-pizza team) with a coaching engagement lasting 3 months. Ok, this is a lie. There is no “industry standard,” but in conversations with many a seasoned coach we tended to agree that this was a reasonable compromise between the GO-GO-GO of upper management and the realistic needs of the teams on the ground
  • Take a group of thought-leaders to Agile India or LeanKanbanIndia
  • Train your teams: I’ve seen companies pay for 2-day Scrum Master classes. At $1,200 USD this gets pretty pricey when you are multiplying by hundreds. We chose a more frugal approach and created our own 1-day class. And here I got lucky— I found an internal trainer, Prakash Rananavare, who really understands Scrum. And he’s as good a trainer as I’ve ever met. We worked as a pair for much of the training, with the LEGO4SCRUM game at the end of the day. Damn, those were fun times. We trained 600 people in 6 months. I’m not recommending this. It’s just what we did to keep the ball moving. There was a lot of momentum behind the “new way of working” and people wanted the training. Teams were flipping to Scrum without coaches. We said, “Do it! You don’t need to ask permission!”
  • Yeah. This is a little risky. I know. Shitty agile teams can tarnish the agile rep. Thankfully, I didn’t have to deal with much blowback. Perhaps I just got lucky

Don’t miss part 2 — which starts with “Scaling agile without DevOps is a disaster”

If you liked this, click the💚 below so other people will see this here on Medium.

item.title: Part 2 of Lessons Learned from Two Years in India as Agile & DevOps Transformation Lead for a Very…
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
Part 2 of Lessons Learned from Two Years in India as Agile & DevOps Transformation Lead for a Very Big MNC

Step 4: Start Your DevOps Journey

  • Scaling agile without DevOps is a nightmare. I was lucky enough to be part of a leadership team that knew this. The reason you have this highly structured waterfall system is to ensure quality in a world of largely manual processes. If you keep all those manual processes (testing, environments, config management, deployment) you will always need all the stage-gating, all those gate keepers, and the Herculean efforts surrounding release weekend. Your x-functional Scrum teams will be pulled apart by the gravity of the release cycle (read Leading the Transformation or watch Jez’ Stop Scaling Agile presentation)
  • Create a DevOps SWAT team to start automation and tool training for the masses — recruit your best keeners who are passionate about this tech and do whatever it takes to keep them happy. They will burn the midnight oil for you if you give them fun work to do. My team went to a week-long bootcamp on CI/CD and Mule. Then returned to teach about 80 engineers what they learned. This gives you the BW to scale your DevOps initiatives.
  • Big shout-out to Shailendra and Sandesh for being leaders in every way — my time in India would have been radically diminished without you two!
  • Note: It was not uncommon to have people working until late in the night. It was not a death march, not compulsory, but part of the hardworking culture. It’s also a function of traffic patterns. If you leave the office before 18:30 you’re good. If you leave anytime btw 18:30–21:30 you will be in gridlock for a long time. My 8km commute could take 1.5 hrs during monsoon and regularly took an hour
  • Recognize this DevOps team in front of their peers and showcase their work when big execs come to town. They are doing plumbing work. It’s not glamorous unless you bring the party. We made a 5 minute video set to Indian dance pop music and broadcast it far and wide. Everyone loved it — execs in our LOB, execs outside our LOB, devs from all regions — that was a lot of fun. This team also presented at internal, massive un-conferences and their booth was one of the most popular
  • Get a “CIO mandate” to do crazy shit, such as automating mainframe deployment. The tools are there to do it. All it takes is vision and commitment…and loads of coordination and communication…but if the CIO is behind it, the rest of IT will come alongside. Note: Your CIO will love your audacious proposal. When you succeed, it’s a nice feather in her cap to show her bosses and the business

Step 5: Ensuring and Extending Change

  • Every expat’s biggest fear is that the day after you leave, things go right back to the old ways. I began working on my succession planning six months into my two-year gig. That meant finding the talent, clearing it with their bosses, begin building community, and then building a Coaches-in-Training program
  • With this group of internal coaches we created cultural learning events that would be repeated weekly and, after a couple of months, led by the internal staff
  • Think Fridays! are 30 minute agile learning games on the floor in the late afternoon. We use a Nepalese prayer bell to get butts out of chairs. It’s a nice bell — handcrafted, purchased by one of our team from a local artisan. This is pretty splashy behaviour for office culture in any country; here it was just absurd. I found that I had to be a bit over-the-top and channel my inner Jimmy Fallon to make these events successful. This freed up the coaches-in-training to imitate me later — it wasn’t pretentious for them to be outrageous, they were, in a sense, mocking me with everyone getting to laugh
  • Lesson: I found my teams to be rather conservative/quiet in public. For instance, it was often hard to get people to speak up when you asked the crowd a question. I took to calling on people by name. That works pretty well. They all have opinions, but there’s a social deference at work that will keep them quiet unless you pull answers from the crowd
  • During one set of workshops I asked the 30 person gathering direct questions such as, “Who saw Ganesh (BIG Boss) speak today? Come on. Raise your hand.” No one raised their hands. I had five workshops and less than 5 people raised their hand. Afterwards I checked with the event leader who said, “Man, we had 90% of the building in attendance for Ganesh’s talk. I think you just experienced a ‘cultural difference!’”
  • YouTube Mondays — these were so much fun, I decided to write another article on this
  • Bi-monthly LEGO4SCRUM game for new joiners — 2.5 hours in the middle of the floor, led by the coaches. In India there’s a lot of turnover. What better way to welcome new teammates?
  • Awards like “Just Do It” that I copied from Amazon
  • Team led floor presentations to show off new DevOps tech or successful (or failed!) agile projects
  • Agile Labs — “block booked” meeting rooms in the middle of the floor for daily standups. No one could book them through Exchange. They had to signup on the whiteboard in the room. 20 minute slots. This gives each team a home with a regular meet time. Note 20 min slots and not 30. Yes. Very intentional
  • Note: Meeting rooms were political territory. They are in short supply and possession of one confers power. The rest of the floor is “open office” design so having privacy = importance. Some local bosses take pride in having the biggest mtg room for any/every meeting. Visiting execs use the second nicest (or the nicest!) as their personal office. Getting a couple of these converted to agile labs was a true coup
  • Agile corners — we had too many standup teams to accommodate in the labs, so we got portable flat screens and whiteboards in the corners and in the cafe to expand our capability

Final Thoughts

  • Only time will tell if my two years of work brought lasting change. Organizational entropy is a real thing. Failure appears to be the norm in big IT agile xforms.
  • But I’m hopeful. The teams I worked with were pretty amazing. Loads of talent. Embracing change. Leadership is all aboard. Fundamental changes to HR roles and TOM are in place. As one exec said, “We burned the boat. There’s no going back.” There’s other reasons I’m hopeful, but I’ll have to write about those later
  • I was invited, last minute and rather haphazardly, to a review of one of our key agile programs in my last month there. I was an afterthought because the teams were operating completely autonomously. They were showing a “before and after our Scrum tune up” picture of the teams’ performance/quality and the numbers were compelling — and legit (there’s a lot of lying in this business). These amazing people were becoming rock-stars with their business sponsors, and I had no part in it. I couldn’t have been happier

If you liked this, click the💚 below so other people will see this here on Medium.

In case you missed part 1, it’s here

item.title: Lessons for Starting an Agile Library and Creating a Reading Culture in India for Your MNC
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
Getting our team on a regular diet of reading was key to generating excitement about a new way of working. Learning how other companies are succeeding creates natural competition. Plus, our folks love ideas and chatting about them. Here are a few tricks to help you. Further down is my list of “must reads” and crowd favourites in Pune

  1. Buy the books yourself — your colleagues might be rich (family land) or scraping by (4 mouths, 1 income) but they will all be frugal. It is (understandably) part of the culture. No worries. You can play librarian. Consider doing monthly expense reports to cover this and other purchases (e.g. post-it notes, markers, LEGO, prizes) as part of your servant-leadership role. As my colleagues said numerous times, “Boss won’t refuse your expenses. But mine…not sure”
  2. Make the bookshelf very visible — I had them stacked next to my desk and shoved them in people’s faces, saying, “Have you read this? IT CHANGED MY LIFE” and then had a story to back it up. You can’t do that with ebooks
  3. Have a signout system— books will disappear into the ether otherwise (as they do in any country but in India your teams are likely so large, your chance of recovering a book without records is nil)
  4. Put a tight time limit on checkouts — I had a 1 week policy that always got stretched to 2, but after 2 wks the likelihood of a book being read drops precipitously
  5. Walk around with your current reading material in hand. Sit in a prominent space and read in public. During work hours. Tell other to do the same. We had a very big exec who regularly told the troops to “invest 30 mins to an hour a day in yourself, your career. You will be surprised how fast it adds up.” I’d always quote him and remind people that he’s telling you to think of your careers in a new way — you are not low-cost factory workers; you are high-value knowledge workers who can compete with any region
  6. Make sure your management team and leaders are always reading something. Ask them, What are you reading this week? If they are stuck, help them find a book and then buy it for them. They will almost certainly read it straight away. In the US this might not work, but Indians take gift-giving very seriously. It’s one of the many awesome things about life here

Books

  • Leading the Transformation, Gruver & Mouser — 104 pages of awesome. Most important book I read. Based on HP Firmware transformation achieving a 10x improvement. I bought over a dozen copies. Handed them out like candy
  • Scrum: The Art of Doing Twice the Work in Half the Time, by the Sutherlands, is a best-seller for good reason. I read it twice and then got the audible version. It’s just that good — punchy, real-world, no bullshit. Handed it out to several execs as well
  • Software in 30 Days, by Schwaber and Sutherland, another great book by the masters, this one is easily consumed by your business peers and sets the rationale for agile transformation in business terms: “agility is an organization’s ability to meet a market opportunity.” Now, who doesn’t want to be agile?
  • Agile IT Agile IT Organization Design: For Digital Transformation and Continuous Delivery, by Sriram Narayan, is a must-read for CXOs looking to reshape their organizations for the 21st c. This tome was passed from leader to leader in our org and fundamentally shaped our TOM strategy
  • Continuous Delivery: Reliable Software Releases through Build, Test, and Deployment Automation, by Jez and David, is a classic and a must-read for our engineering leads
  • Decisive: How to Make Better Choices in Life and Work, by Chip and Dan Heath, will give you teaching material for your troops about decision-making processes, biases, and strategies for improving the culture on the floor. Many big IT shops are stuck in 90’s era thinking. Use this material in some Town Halls and get people talking
  • Switch: How to Change Things When Change Is Hard, by Chip and Dan Heath. Ditto for this one. The Rider, Elephant, and Path metaphor is sticky and will become part of your office parlance
  • Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us, by Dan Pink. Autonomy, Mastery, & Purpose are the motivational tools for the creative class. Our leadership team loved this book and the teams loved Dan’s TED talks
  • Lean Enterprise: How High Performance Organizations Innovate at Scale, by Jez, Joanne, and Barry, was written, as Jez says, to show CIOs how to implement what was described in Continuous Delivery
  • The Lean Startup: How Today’s Entrepreneurs Use Continuous Innovation to Create Radically Successful Businesses, by Eric Ries, will introduce a variety of entrepreneurial concepts to your teams — the importance of experimentation, requirements as hypotheses, vanity metrics. My favourite vanity metric in agile transformation — “number of projects running agile.” What is a project? What is agile? What’s our goal here?
  • The DevOps Handbook: How to Create World-Class Agility, Reliability, and Security in Technology Organizations, by Gene, Jez, John, John, and Pat, is a comprehensive handbook that your leadership team and keeners alike will enjoy
  • The Power of Habit: Why We Do What We do in Life and Business, by Duhigg. One IT exec translated the “zero workplace injury” idea from ALCOA into a “zero defect” cornerstone quality program. I thought he was nuts at first, but when I connected the dots, that defects are like injuries — hygiene indicators of workplace sloppiness, poorly engaged workers — I could get on board. The book is chock full of insights, thoughtfully delivered, easily translatable into town halls
  • The Second Machine Age: Work Progress And Prosperity In A Time Of Brilliant Technologies, by Brynjolfsson & McAfee. Just because. It’s fun and full of great ideas. Remember, a big part of your job is to nurture a culture of ideas

If you liked this, click the💚 below so other people will see this here on Medium.

item.title: Lessons Learned: Building Agile Coaching Capability in India
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
Did you know that if you have been a Scrum Master or even a member of a Scrum team in India for just one project, you can throw “Coach” on your CV and get a 50% pay bump? It’s true. And if you are going to hire some good coaches in India, I have some lessons for you

  1. There are 3 levels of coaches: shit, junior, and expert. Assuming you can weed out the first, you will find junior coaches (couple of years of agile coaching experience, 5 years scrum, CSP) through large GSVs such as Wipro and CapGem. They cost ~$450 USD per day. They range from poor to decent to great at working with local teams, leading agile learning events, and doing assessments. It took me 3 months to find my first good junior. She turned out to be a rock-star who organized the rest of the coaches while I dealt with senior management. Lesson: Don’t underestimate your “juniors” — I found several of them who were fantastic
  2. The experts start at $1,000/day. That’s not a typo — that’s market rate. And that’s for independents. Go to a large global agile consultancy and you will pay $1,800/day. Yeah. No one in London believed me either. They told me I should quit and go get one of those gigs. “I’m thinking about it.” You’ll want at least one expert coach on your team to deal with senior management and on-shore leadership
  3. You will have a hard time hiring that expert. You can get approval from, say the Global CIO in writing, but the local guy who pulls the trigger on the contract will balk. His boss will kill him if he brings in someone this expensive. CIOs come and go, but this guy and his boss are survivors, and survivors don’t spend money like this
  4. Expect to wait 3 months to on-board new coaches if they are currently employed elsewhere. That’s pretty standard math in India. BGV takes a while and standard notice periods are crazy long
  5. Expect your really good, junior coaches to leave you in 6 months. They will have great job offers in Europe or big promotions in-country. You will not be able to compete
  6. Get started very early on creating an internal coach-in-training (CIT) program. You have no idea when someone “upstairs” will cut your budget nor can you plan to keep those great junior coaches
  7. I’d recommend a sort of Shu-Ha-Ri internal coaching curriculum. About 6–12 months for each stage. In addition to active coaching of new teams, each stage has a list of activities/achievements such as leading LEGO games, hosting learning events, facilitating team self-assessments, hosting a Town Hall or debate on an agile topic, attending an industry conference such as Agile India. This gives the internal staff a longer term vision and avoids the tendency to dumb-down the expectations for “internal certifications.” I want our internal coaches to be able to walk across the street and get a legit coaching gig at Barclays or IBM
  8. Note: Getting approval to go to a conference is a big deal here — bosses tend to dismiss them as boondoggles and the old “low-cost factory” mentality is alive and well in many managers — so putting confs in the curriculum forces management to approve the travel request — smart, eh?

Getting a team of great coaches in place is crucial for transformation. They bring a skillset that is probably in very short supply among your team, and they bring an outsider’s perspective. Your team needs to see the “new way of working” modelled on a daily basis. Having coaches also brings a great deal of energy to the transformation. The teams know that senior management is serious — they are spending money! — and this helps the staff take risks and try new things. I am so grateful for my coaching team — without them, I would have been hooped. They were caring, thoughtful, articulate, well-read, hard-working professionals whom I consider friends. Thanks team for all your hard work

Lesson Learned: Get the CIT program goals into the aspiring coaches’ HR performance management plan. I don’t know how you manage your people, but in a very large MNC (and especially in India), the perf mgmt doc and your annual review has more power than you and your fancy talk. A lot more. When push-comes to shove, your CIT program will suffer when their line manager’s pet project needs manpower

If you liked this, click the💚 below so other people will see this here on Medium.

item.title: So You’re Going to be an Expat in India?
item.dc:creator: Matt Everard
item.content:encoded:
On March 31, 2015, we arrived in Pune on a two-year secondment (Brit fancy term for “gig”) and a massive amount of learning. As we wrap up our time here, I’ve captured some Do’s and Don’ts for newbies:

  • There is no “3 second” food-on-ground rule here — if it hits the floor, put it in the bin
  • Your hands are dirty. Always. Don’t rub your eye, pick your nose, or teeth
  • Let sleeping dogs lie — literally you will understand this saying in a new way
  • Smuggle in as much booze and wine as you can fit in your luggage on every trip out of country
  • Stay heads down at customs, move quickly, quietly say, “These are not the droids you are looking for”
  • Bring cured meat and cheese in your luggage along with wine. You laugh now, but once here, you’ll see the genius of it
  • Throw parties with other expats when you return with those Iberian hams and Priorat wine. You will be much loved
  • Watch Million Dollar Arm
  • Read Shantaram (listen haters — it’s a good book)
  • Your driver does not work on Sunday (even though his company says he does)
  • You will overpay your maid — and your stay-at-home spouse will undoubtedly complain about her
  • Make a family rule: No complaining about the maid
  • Don’t eat out “like a local”- you are not a local
  • Contrary to popular wisdom, our intestines did *not* acclimate in 2 years
  • Unless you can see the ocean, skip the seafood
  • Always buy VIP movie tickets (like first class airplane seats)
  • Never stay at 1–3 star hotels
  • Get a water cooler on day 1
  • Don’t drink the RO water (no minerals)
  • No returns allowed — once purchased, it is yours. Period. No matter what they tell you
  • Everything can be delivered
  • Buy meat from the Hyatt — not local grocery stores (single-chain of refrigeration custody)
  • Expect to spend way more for western quality in every category of goods
  • Scan all your important docs and put them on Dropbox before you leave — you will be shocked at the amount of paperwork involved in your stay
  • Get a scanning app for your pocket computer
  • Bring your own BBQ — even if you grill irregularly (they are insanely expensive here)
  • Set aside a weekend to procure your gas cylinder (it is considered a weapon)
  • Pack warm clothes for holiday trips
  • Bring racquets and golf clubs (but that’s about all for sporting equip)
  • Buy unlocked iPhone on Amazon here (not in the mall)
  • Buy everything at Amazon. Avoid the malls
  • Get Hotsar (select HBO titles for $4/mo)
  • Pack Sonos speakers (220V compatible)
  • Bring good kitchen tools
  • Clothes are cheaper and better at a Banana Republic sale in the States
  • You never walk anywhere — sidewalks (when present) are motorbike lines
  • Good chocolate is rare
  • Daily supply of protein is a constant struggle (would you like chicken or chicken?)
  • Don’t get a dog
  • Consider buying an Enfield
  • Refresh your mac hardware before leaving
  • Buy Phillips air purifiers
  • Give your driver extra money — he’s living on $200USD per month
  • Tip as often and as generously as you want. Everyone in food service is broke (living on less than your driver)
